critic_raw: The sequence model uses 'merchant embeddings learned from co-occurrence patterns in the training set using Word2Vec.' Word2Vec embeddings for merchants learned on training data may not generalize to new merchants that appear only in the validation and test windows. New merchants in months 13-18 would have out-of-vocabulary merchant IDs, receiving random or zero embeddings. This could systematically disadvantage the sequence model for new merchant transactions in the test period.
defender_raw: Round 1: New merchants in the test period would receive the unknown/OOV token embedding — a learned representation for unseen merchants, or a zero vector if not explicitly handled. This is a deployment limitation of learned embeddings. However, the gradient boosting baseline doesn't learn merchant embeddings — it uses merchant category (MCC) features as categorical variables, which are much more stable (MCCs are standardized industry codes). The sequence model's embedding disadvantage for new merchants is acknowledged; an ablation testing 'sequence model without learned merchant embeddings' is explicitly included in the confound controls: 'Train an ablated sequence model without learned merchant embeddings to test if one-hot merchants suffice.' This ablation directly tests whether merchant embeddings or the sequence structure drives performance.

rounds.critic_followup: The ablation 'without learned merchant embeddings' would test one-hot merchants as an alternative. But one-hot encoding of merchant IDs in a test window with new merchants would also have OOV issues — a one-hot dimension doesn't exist for new merchants. How does the ablation handle this?
rounds.defender_followup: For one-hot encoding with new merchants, the standard approach is to map unknown merchant IDs to an 'unknown merchant' category. This is equivalent to using 'channel diversity' and 'unique merchant count' as aggregated features, which the gradient boosting baseline already uses. The ablation is testing whether sequence structure (ordering, timing) adds value over one-hot or aggregated merchant features. New merchant OOV is handled consistently across all representations. The gradient boosting baseline handles this via aggregated features (unique merchant count, geographic diversity) which are stable features even for new merchants.

adjudication_raw: One concern: new merchant OOV for Word2Vec embeddings in test period. Addressed: OOV handling is standard; explicit ablation tests whether merchant embeddings vs. sequence structure drives performance. Verdict: defense_wins.
